name: Chocolate (all types)
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, panting, excessive thirst and urination, hyperactivity, abnormal heart rhythm, tremors, seizures
mechanism: Contains methylxanthines, found in cacao seeds. Darker chocolate and cocoa powder have the highest concentration; white chocolate the lowest.
action: Contact your veterinarian or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) immediately, especially for dark chocolate or large amounts.

name: Xylitol
symptoms: Vomiting, lethargy, loss of coordination, progressing to seizures; elevated liver enzymes and liver failure can appear within days
mechanism: Found in sugar-free gum, candy, baked goods, toothpaste, and some peanut butter. Causes a rapid insulin release leading to dangerously low blood sugar.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care immediately, even for small amounts. Always check ingredient labels on peanut butter and sugar-free products.

name: Alcohol (any alcoholic beverage or food containing alcohol)
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, lack of coordination, difficulty breathing, tremors, changes in blood pH, coma
mechanism: Rapidly absorbed after ingestion. Under no circumstances should pets be given alcohol.
action: Contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately -- prompt action matters since alcohol is absorbed quickly.

name: Grapes and Raisins
symptoms: Vomiting, lethargy, decreased appetite, signs of kidney failure (may appear 24-48 hours after ingestion)
mechanism: The specific toxic substance is not definitively known, but even small amounts have caused kidney failure in dogs. There is no known safe amount.
action: Contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately, even if only a small amount was eaten.

name: Macadamia Nuts
symptoms: Weakness, incoordination (especially in the hind legs), depression, vomiting, tremors, elevated body temperature
mechanism: The toxic mechanism is not fully understood. Symptoms typically appear within 12 hours and usually resolve within 24-72 hours.
action: Contact your veterinarian, particularly if your dog ate a large quantity or symptoms appear.

name: Onion (raw, cooked, powdered, or dehydrated)
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, pale gums, elevated heart rate (signs of anemia may take days to appear)
mechanism: Member of the Allium family. Damages red blood cells, which can lead to anemia. Powdered/dehydrated forms are more concentrated and dangerous.
action: Contact your veterinarian, especially with powdered onion or larger amounts -- symptoms of anemia can be delayed.

name: Garlic (raw, cooked, or powdered)
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, pale gums, elevated heart rate
mechanism: Also a member of the Allium family, similar mechanism to onion. Considered more potent than onion by weight.
action: Contact your veterinarian, especially for concentrated forms like garlic powder or large amounts.

name: Chives
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, red blood cell damage in larger amounts
mechanism: Another Allium species with the same red-blood-cell-damaging mechanism as onion and garlic.
action: Contact your veterinarian if a significant amount was eaten.

name: Avocado
symptoms: Vomiting and diarrhea in dogs (much more severe in birds, rabbits, and livestock)
mechanism: Contains persin in the leaves, fruit, seeds, and bark. Dogs are relatively less sensitive than birds or rabbits, but ingestion can still cause GI upset.
action: Monitor for vomiting or diarrhea; contact your vet if symptoms are severe or persist.

name: Coffee, Tea, and Caffeine
symptoms: Vomiting, restlessness, rapid breathing, abnormal heart rhythm, tremors, seizures
mechanism: Contains methylxanthines, the same toxic compound family as chocolate.
action: Contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately, especially for concentrated forms like coffee grounds or espresso.

name: Milk and Dairy Products
symptoms: Diarrhea, gas, and other digestive upset
mechanism: Dogs generally lack sufficient lactase to properly digest lactose in dairy products.
action: Not usually an emergency; discontinue feeding and monitor. Contact your vet if symptoms are severe or persistent.

name: Raw Yeast Dough
symptoms: Bloating, abdominal pain, vomiting, disorientation (from alcohol produced as the yeast ferments)
mechanism: Dough continues to rise in the warm, moist stomach, producing gas that can cause the stomach to bloat and potentially twist -- a life-threatening emergency. Yeast also produces alcohol as it ferments.
action: Contact your veterinarian or seek emergency care immediately -- this can become a true emergency quickly.

name: Raw or Undercooked Meat and Eggs
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea (from bacterial contamination); skin and coat problems with prolonged raw egg feeding
mechanism: Can contain Salmonella or E. coli. Raw eggs also contain avidin, an enzyme that can reduce absorption of biotin over time.
action: Monitor for GI symptoms; contact your vet if vomiting/diarrhea is severe or persistent.

name: Raw or Cooked Bones
symptoms: Choking, vomiting, constipation, or signs of internal injury (straining, pain, lethargy) if a bone splinters
mechanism: Bones can cause obstruction or injury to the gastrointestinal tract, potentially requiring surgery.
action: If your dog is choking, in pain, or shows signs of a blockage, seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

name: Salt and Salty Snack Foods
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, depression, tremors, elevated body temperature, excessive thirst/urination, seizures in severe cases
mechanism: Large amounts of sodium can lead to sodium ion poisoning.
action: Avoid salty snacks like chips and pretzels; contact your vet if your dog consumed a large quantity or shows symptoms.

name: Nuts (almonds, pecans, walnuts, and similar)
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, and potentially pancreatitis with high-fat exposure
mechanism: High oil and fat content can upset digestion and, with large or repeated exposure, contribute to pancreatitis.
action: Avoid feeding; contact your vet if your dog shows ongoing vomiting, abdominal pain, or lethargy.

name: Coconut and Coconut Products
symptoms: Stomach upset, loose stools or diarrhea
mechanism: Coconut flesh and milk contain oils that may cause GI upset in larger amounts. Coconut water is high in potassium and should be avoided.
action: Small amounts are generally not a serious concern; use caution and monitor for digestive upset.

name: Nutmeg
symptoms: Vomiting, dry mouth, agitation, dilated or constricted pupils, rapid heart rate, difficulty walking, hallucinations in large amounts
mechanism: Contains myristicin, a compound with strongest effects at high doses or in small dogs.
action: Contact your veterinarian if your dog ingests any amount of nutmeg.

name: Cinnamon
symptoms: Mild stomach upset with small amounts; with larger amounts, irritation of the mouth/throat, hyperactivity, elevated heart rate. Inhaled cinnamon powder can cause coughing and breathing difficulty.
mechanism: Generally less dangerous than nutmeg, but can irritate the digestive tract and airway.
action: Monitor for GI upset; contact your vet if breathing difficulty or significant symptoms occur.

name: Wild Mushrooms
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, lethargy, jaundice, seizures, or organ failure depending on species ingested
mechanism: Many wild mushroom species are toxic and can cause severe liver failure or death; store-bought washed mushrooms are a much lower risk.
action: Treat any wild mushroom ingestion as an emergency -- contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately, and bring a photo or sample of the mushroom if possible.

name: Fruit Pits and Seeds (cherries, peaches, plums, apricots)
symptoms: Choking or gastrointestinal obstruction; cyanide poisoning is rare but possible if pits are chewed/crushed in large quantities
mechanism: Pits from the Prunus genus contain compounds that release cyanide only if crushed or chewed extensively. The bigger everyday risk is physical obstruction or choking.
action: Contact your veterinarian if your dog swallowed a pit whole (choking/obstruction risk) or chewed up a large quantity.

name: Human Pain Relievers (ibuprofen, acetaminophen, aspirin)
symptoms: Vomiting, stomach ulcers, lethargy, difficulty breathing, liver or kidney damage
mechanism: These medications are formulated for human metabolism and can be highly toxic to dogs even at doses that seem small.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care immediately -- never give human pain medication to a dog without explicit veterinary direction.

name: Human Antidepressants and Cold Medicines
symptoms: Tremors, seizures, elevated heart rate, agitation
mechanism: Even small doses of many human psychiatric or cold medications can be harmful to dogs.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care immediately, and bring the medication packaging if possible.

name: Bleach and Disinfectants
symptoms: Burns in the mouth and throat, drooling, vomiting, difficulty breathing
mechanism: Corrosive chemicals that can damage tissue on contact.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care immediately. Do not induce vomiting unless directed by a professional.

name: Laundry and Dishwashing Detergents (especially pods)
symptoms: Vomiting, lethargy, difficulty breathing, chemical burns if bitten into
mechanism: Concentrated detergent pods are especially dangerous if punctured by a dog's teeth.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

name: Grape Vines / Plants
symptoms: Same as grapes/raisins: vomiting, lethargy, signs of kidney failure
mechanism: The whole plant carries the same risk as the fruit itself.
action: Treat as an emergency and contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately, same as for grapes or raisins.

name: Protein Bars and Protein Drinks
symptoms: Varies by ingredient -- often contain xylitol, chocolate, or high sugar/fat content
mechanism: A common source of accidental xylitol or chocolate exposure since owners may not realize these products can contain them.
action: Check the ingredient label immediately; if xylitol or chocolate is present, contact your veterinarian right away.

name: Cooked Onion Dishes (onion rings, soups, sauces)
symptoms: Same as raw onion: vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, pale gums
mechanism: Cooking does not remove the toxic compounds in onions -- cooked onion is just as dangerous as raw.
action: Contact your veterinarian if a meaningful amount was eaten, especially in concentrated dishes like soup or gravy.

name: Moldy Food or Garbage
symptoms: Tremors, seizures, vomiting, elevated body temperature
mechanism: Certain molds produce tremorgenic mycotoxins that can cause severe neurological symptoms.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care immediately if your dog has gotten into spoiled food or the garbage and shows tremors or unusual behavior.

name: Raw/Green Potatoes and Green Tomatoes
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy in large amounts
mechanism: Unripe or green parts of the nightshade family contain solanine, which is reduced significantly by cooking and ripening.
action: Avoid feeding raw or green parts; contact your vet if a large amount was eaten or symptoms appear.

name: Citrus (lemons, limes, grapefruit -- peels, seeds, and pith especially)
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, and in large amounts, sensitivity to light
mechanism: Citrus fruits contain compounds like psoralens and essential oils, concentrated most in the peel, seeds, and pith.
action: Small amounts of citrus flesh are usually not a major concern; contact your vet if peel/seeds were eaten in quantity or symptoms are notable.

name: High-Fat Table Scraps (fatty meat trimmings, bacon grease)
symptoms: Vomiting, abdominal pain, lethargy -- potential signs of pancreatitis
mechanism: High-fat foods can trigger pancreatitis, a painful and potentially serious inflammation of the pancreas.
action: Avoid feeding fatty scraps; contact your vet if your dog shows abdominal pain, vomiting, or lethargy after eating fatty food.

name: Candy and Gum (general, unspecified ingredients)
symptoms: Varies -- may contain chocolate, xylitol, or high sugar content
mechanism: A common accidental exposure category; always check for chocolate or xylitol specifically since those are the truly dangerous ingredients.
action: Check ingredients for chocolate or xylitol immediately; if either is present, treat as a high-danger exposure and contact your vet.

name: Energy Drinks
symptoms: Vomiting, restlessness, rapid heart rate, tremors, seizures
mechanism: Very high caffeine content, same methylxanthine mechanism as coffee and chocolate, often in concentrated amounts.
action: Contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately.

name: Beer and Fermented Beverages
symptoms: Vomiting, disorientation, difficulty breathing, coma in severe cases
mechanism: Contains alcohol, absorbed rapidly; same risk profile as other alcoholic beverages.
action: Contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately.

name: Moldy Walnuts (specifically)
symptoms: Tremors, seizures, vomiting
mechanism: Walnuts that have gone moldy can harbor tremorgenic mycotoxins in addition to the general risks of nuts.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care immediately if your dog has eaten moldy nuts of any kind and shows neurological symptoms.

name: Concentrated Essential Oils (e.g. used in baking, like peppermint or tea tree oil)
symptoms: Vomiting, drooling, weakness, tremors, difficulty breathing
mechanism: Highly concentrated oils can be toxic even in small amounts, distinct from the whole herb or spice.
action: Contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately if concentrated essential oil was ingested.

name: Pizza Dough or Any Uncooked Bread Product
symptoms: Bloating, disorientation, vomiting, abdominal pain
mechanism: Same risk as raw yeast dough generally -- continues to rise and ferment in the stomach.
action: Contact your veterinarian or seek emergency care immediately, especially if your dog appears bloated or distressed.

name: Avocado Pit (specifically, as an object)
symptoms: Choking, vomiting, signs of intestinal blockage (straining, lethargy, loss of appetite)
mechanism: Separate from persin toxicity, the pit itself is a significant choking and obstruction hazard due to its size and shape.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care if your dog swallowed an avocado pit or shows any signs of blockage.

name: Corn on the Cob
symptoms: Vomiting, signs of intestinal blockage (straining, lethargy, loss of appetite, abdominal pain)
mechanism: The cob itself is not digestible and is a common cause of intestinal obstruction, often requiring surgery.
action: Seek emergency veterinary care if your dog has eaten part or all of a corn cob.

name: Sugar-Free Gum (general)
symptoms: Vomiting, lethargy, loss of coordination, seizures
mechanism: The vast majority of sugar-free gum contains xylitol -- treat any sugar-free gum ingestion as a xylitol exposure unless confirmed otherwise.
action: Contact your veterinarian or poison control immediately, and check the label for xylitol if possible.

name: Potato Plant (leaves and stems)
symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy in large amounts
mechanism: Part of the nightshade family; leaves and stems contain more solanine than the tuber itself.
action: Prevent access to potato plants in gardens; contact your vet if a large amount was consumed.
